did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) classifiers, optimized by Lion Optimization Algorithm (LOA) and Monkey Search Algorithm (MSA), for the classification of melanoma skin cancer images using GLCM features.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving GLCM feature extraction, feature selection using Lion Optimization Algorithm (LOA) and Monkey Search Algorithm (MSA), and classification using LSTM models on the ISIC dataset. It reports specific performance metrics (e.g., 97.49% accuracy for LOA-LSTM) and includes sections on Materials and Methods, Results, and Discussion, characterizing it as a technical study rather than a review.

title: Performance Analysis of LSTM Classification Model from GLCM with Lion Optimization Algorithm Features for Melanoma Identification from Skin Cancer Images
journal: 2024 International Conference on Computing and Intelligent Reality Technologies (ICCIRT)
brief_summary: This study addresses the early detection of melanoma skin cancer by analyzing the performance of Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) classifiers optimized with Lion Optimization Algorithm (LOA) and Monkey Search Algorithm (MSA). The researchers utilized the ISIC dataset, extracting Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) features from skin lesion images. Feature selection was performed using LOA and MSA to identify the most discriminative texture patterns before classification. The models were evaluated using a 70/30 train-test split with ten-fold cross-validation, assessing metrics such as accuracy, error rate, and Jaccard Index. The LOA-based LSTM classifier achieved the highest accuracy of 97.49%, outperforming the MSA-based classifier which reached 95.89%. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of combining GLCM features with LOA optimization for improving melanoma identification.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on the detection and classification of melanoma skin cancer using the ISIC dataset, which contains labeled melanoma images. The experiments specifically target melanoma identification.

all_task_types.target: Melanoma skin cancer identification (binary classification of malignant melanoma vs. other skin lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'classification of skin melanoma cancer' and 'melanoma identification'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) using LSTM classifiers optimized by LOA and MSA on GLCM features. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as Accuracy, Error Rate, Jaccard Index, Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC), and Fowlkes-Mallows Index (FM), which rely on True Positives, True Negatives, False Positives, and False Negatives.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Database (SIIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification)
datasets_used.description: One of the largest and most renowned databases for skin cancer research, consisting of more than 36,000 high-resolution skin lesion images (typically 1024x1024 pixels, resized to 224x224 for the model) including a considerable portion of labeled melanoma images. Accessed via Kaggle.

use.training: 70% of the dataset was used for training the LSTM classifiers.
use.validation: Ten-fold cross-validation was applied to the training data.
use.testing: 30% of the dataset was used for testing the classifiers.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Applied to the training data to evaluate the performance of the LSTM classifiers optimized by LOA and MSA.
Used to divide the ISIC dataset into training (70%) and testing (30%) subsets for model evaluation.
Used to assess the linear dependence and relationship between GLCM features (e.g., Contrast, Correlation, Energy, Homogeneity, Dissimilarity, Entropy) in the dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

ISIC Database (SIIM-ISIC Melanoma Classi.experiment: GLCM Feature Extraction with LOA and MSA Optimization for LSTM Classification
ISIC Database (SIIM-ISIC Melanoma Classi.notes: The LOA-based LSTM classifier outperformed the MSA-based classifier across all reported metrics. The LOA model achieved a higher accuracy (97.49% vs 95.89%) and lower error rate (2.55% vs 4.28%). The paper notes that the LOA-based model shows 'huge superiority' in classification performance.

mathematical_formulation.input_gate: i(t) = σ(Wi * g(t) + Hi * h(t-1) + Bi)
mathematical_formulation.forget_gate: f(t) = σ(Wf * g(t) + Hf * h(t-1) + Bf)
mathematical_formulation.cell_state: c(t) = f(t) * c(t-1) + i(t) * tanh(Wc * g(t) + Hc * h(t-1) + Bc)
mathematical_formulation.output_gate: o(t) = σ(Wo * g(t) + Ho * h(t-1) + Bo)

training_details.optimizer: Lion Optimization Algorithm (LOA) and Monkey Search Algorithm (MSA) used for feature selection, not explicitly stated as the gradient optimizer for LSTM weights
training_details.loss_function: Loss minimization defined as: Loss = (1/T) * Σ[(Y(t) - Ŷ(t))^2]

classification_or_output_head.output_layer: Linear output layer with weight matrix WY and bias BY

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images from 1024x1024 pixels to 224x224 pixels to comply with deep learning model input size
Application of a median filter to smooth out unwanted noise while retaining important edges and textures
conclusions: The LOA-based LSTM classifier achieved an accuracy of 97.49% with an error rate of 2.55%, outperforming the MSA-based LSTM classifier which achieved 95.89% accuracy and a 4.28% error rate.

The LOA-based model demonstrated superior performance across all evaluated metrics, including Jaccard Index (91.29% vs 89.28%), Matthews Correlation Coefficient (0.9398 vs 0.9012), and Fowlkes-Mallows Index (0.9589 vs 0.9199).

Combining Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) feature extraction with Lion Optimization Algorithm (LOA) for feature selection effectively enhances the classification performance for melanoma identification.
The study illustrates the promise of machine learning approaches, specifically those utilizing optimized feature selection, in promoting the early detection of malignant melanoma.
GLCM features such as correlation, energy, and homogeneity provide useful texture information for distinguishing skin cancer patterns, with strong correlations observed between certain feature pairs.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single dataset (ISIC) accessed via Kaggle without reporting external validation on independent cohorts, limiting generalizability.
The paper does not report the specific number of images per class (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) or the exact train/validation/test split counts, only the 70/30 ratio.

Feature selection was performed using Lion Optimization Algorithm (LOA) and Monkey Search Algorithm (MSA) on the training data; the paper does not explicitly state if this process was repeated within each fold of the ten-fold cross-validation, raising potential data leakage risks if feature selection statistics were derived from the entire dataset prior to splitting.
The LSTM architecture is applied to static GLCM texture features rather than sequential image data, which is an unconventional use of recurrent networks for this type of input.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to compare the performance of the LOA-based and MSA-based models; differences are described qualitatively as 'huge superiority'.
The study is a conference paper with limited scope, acknowledging that increasing the dataset size and using ensemble learning are necessary for future clinical applicability.
Preprocessing included resizing images to 224x224 and applying a median filter, but no data augmentation techniques were employed.
